coredata.dc:title: Simple synthesis of water-soluble ruthenium carry CDs as admirable probe for improved environment and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Annamalai, Arun
Annamalai, Kumaresan
Ravichandran, Ramya
Anilkumar, A.K.
M R, Ganesh
Elumalai, Sundaravadivel
coredata.dc:description: Metal-doped carbon dots (M-CDs) are brand-new 0-D nanomaterial that affords multiple guaranteed properties because of metals in between tiny carbon matrices. However, preparing M-CDs without aggregation of metals is very challenging. Here, we proposed a new tactic method to prepare highly water-soluble ruthenium Ru(Ⅱ)-holding CDs (Ru@CDs) through a one-step microwave-assisted approach with quantum yield of 19.21% using ruthenium chloride and ethylene diamine without any solvents. The optical, surface, structural, and morphological features were deeply evaluated with the support of UV–vis, photoluminescence, Fourier-transform infrared, Raman, X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscope, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques. The excellent photoluminescent and water-soluble properties of Ru@CDs were employed to individually detection of dual carcinogenic environmental toxic metal ions through the photoluminescence technique. They detected the lower limit of 0.128 μM as Cr6+ and 0.328 μM as Hg2+ in the aqueous environment, which was very low compared to CDs. Followed by Ru@CDs, embedded cost-efficient filter paper was fabricated for real-time visual recognition of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions in water. Significantly, Ru@CDs show good cytotoxicity and effective growth inhibition against cancer cells compared to CDs. So, the Ru@CDs were employed as an effective bioimaging agent selectively. To conclude, facile synthesized Ru@CDs explore its multifunctional quality as a better environmental pollutant detector and bioimaging agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Highly water soluble
Ru-holding CDs
Environmental pollutant
Sensor
Bioimaging
Anticancer activity
Multifunctional behaviors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ru@CDs as a probe for quick detection of environmental pollutants
Detection of individual environmental toxic metal ions in an aqueous medium
Possible assessments for enhancement in detection
Probable mechanism for the distinct metal ions discrimination
Detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ in real water samples
In vitro imaging of cancer cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption of CDs and Ru@CDs. (b–c) Emission spectrum of CDs and Ru@CDs at different wavelength of excitations. (d) Emission maximum of CDs and Ru@CDs with corresponding excitation spectrum. (e–f) Lifetime spectrum of CDs and Ru@CDs.

(a) X-ray diffraction spectra of carbon dots (CDs) and Ru@CDs. (b–c) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CDs with size distribution histogram. (d–e) TEM and high-resolution TEM image of CDs with size distribution histogram.
(a–c) XPS spectrum of Ru@CDs and HR-XPS of C 1s, N 1s, Ru 3d, (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs and Ru@CDs, (e) Raman spectra of CDs and Ru@CDs.

(a) Quenching fluorescence in Ru@CDs with increasing concentrations of Cr6+. (b) Linear fit (I0/I). (c) Quenching emission spectra of Ru@CDs at increasing Hg2+ concentrations. (d) Linear fit (I0/I), respectively.
Illustration of the possible mechanism of Ru@CDs quenching by Cr6+ and Hg2+.
(a)Ru@CDs + Cr6+ and (b)Ru@CDs + Hg2+ in Whatman filter paper.

An MTT results of (THP-1) cells incubated with increasing concentration of CDs and Ru@CDs. CLSM image of (THP-1) cells coincubated with Ru@CDs for 24 h excited at 408 nm at the bright-field (b–c) non-treated cells (d–e) treated cells.

head.ce:title: Simple synthesis of water-soluble ruthenium carry CDs as admirable probe for improved environment and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu, 603203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Interdisciplinary Institute of Indian System of Medicine, SRM IST, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Interdisciplinary Institute of Indian System of Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Interdisciplinary Institute of Indian System of Medicine, SRM IST, Kattankulathur- Tamil Nadu, 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-doped carbon dots (M-CDs) are brand-new 0-D nanomaterial that affords multiple guaranteed properties because of metals in between tiny carbon matrices. However, preparing M-CDs without aggregation of metals is very challenging. Here, we proposed a new tactic method to prepare highly water-soluble ruthenium Ru(Ⅱ)-holding CDs (Ru@CDs) through a one-step microwave-assisted approach with quantum yield of 19.21% using ruthenium chloride and ethylene diamine without any solvents. The optical, surface, structural, and morphological features were deeply evaluated with the support of UV–vis, photoluminescence, Fourier-transform infrared, Raman, X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscope, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy techniques. The excellent photoluminescent and water-soluble properties of Ru@CDs were employed to individually detection of dual carcinogenic environmental toxic metal ions through the photoluminescence technique. They detected the lower limit of 0.128 μM as Cr6+ and 0.328 μM as Hg2+ in the aqueous environment, which was very low compared to CDs. Followed by Ru@CDs, embedded cost-efficient filter paper was fabricated for real-time visual recognition of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions in water. Significantly, Ru@CDs show good cytotoxicity and effective growth inhibition against cancer cells compared to CDs. So, the Ru@CDs were employed as an effective bioimaging agent selectively. To conclude, facile synthesized Ru@CDs explore its multifunctional quality as a better environmental pollutant detector and bioimaging agent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon clusters or carbon dots (CDs) are new, innovative zero-dimensional photonic quantum-sized materials belonging to the carbon family. Collectively, CDs expose countless unusual properties such as excitation-dependent emission, upconverted luminescent, vastly soluble in an aqueous condition, and admirable biocompatibility [1]. The accessible adapting properties make CDs a treasured and affordable material. CDs structure is composed of a homogeneously organized sp2 hybridized crystalline graphitic core followed by a roofed with hydrophilic functional group moieties. These multifunctional and structural properties are improving the concern among researchers and drawing them to exploit several applications from energy and environment to biological domains such as supercapacitors, pollutant sensors, photocatalysts, solar cells, LED, drug delivery, and anticancer agents [2].

Miscellaneous methods are available to synthesize CDs that are hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave irradiation, laser ablation, ultrasonication, pyrolysis, and so on [3]. In contrast, microwave-assisted synthesis methods fall under a bottom-up approach, which is an easy and appropriate method because of the short reaction time, significantly less energy consumption, and simple and safe operation process. Synthesis of CDs with different heteroatoms employed as dopants such as N, S, B, P, and so on, outstandingly tuned their optical, electronic behaviors, and so on, because of the uniform alignment of electronic structure [4]. In contrast to that, metal ions instead of non-metal dopants in CDs skeleton structure significantly stimulate the multifunctional behaviors because the transition metal atom/ions in the CDs matrix have more electrons and vacant orbital in it, which leads to enhance the electron-transfer ability between the metal and CDs, which offers reformed properties that can be applied for multiple applications. Besides, the importance of metal-doped CDs compared to metal complexes of CDs has to been revealed because metal-complexed CDs can quench the fluorescence quality of CDs, which was generally occurred in the quenching mechanisms, even though it affects the fluorescence behavior and also open up the innovative properties [5–7]. On the other side, in metal atom-doped CDs, metal ions are bound to the sp2 hybridized heteroatoms in the place of functionalization of metal ions. So, the metal ion-doped and metal ion-functionalized CDs are most attractive in their perspective. Recently, Liangliang Yue et al. reported one-step ruthenium-containing CDs from the Ru-Aphen complex as a Ru source and citric acid as a carbon source was employed for photodynamic cancer therapy [8]. Limei Chen et al. prepared NCDs and RuCl2 (DMSO)4 through hydrothermal conditions, showing intervalence electron transfer properties between Ru and N in NCDs [9]. Besides, above-reported ruthenium-based CDs are prepared from complex molecule followed by two-step process to achieve Ru2+ state.

Fabricating and the progress of examining devices for real-time sensor applications are very vital for shielding the aqueous environment from carcinogenic pollutants. The heavy metal ions such as hexavalent chromium, divalent mercury, and so on, in groundwater or surface water bodies are perilous candidates for all living things in the world. So, it is necessary to develop excellent, economical, and highly eco-friendly probe material for the detection of heavy metal ions pollutants in an aqueous environment [10,11]. A wide-ranging number of fluorescent probes are available such as metal clusters, quantum dots, polymers, and organic molecules are well-known fluorescence-based materials that are used for the detection of noxious pollutants; the major challenge is that they are all highly toxic and exist in a harmful solvent medium [12]. CDs are one of the best alternative materials compared to toxic materials because CDs have very low toxicity, enhanced photoluminescence, low preparation cost, and are highly soluble in an aqueous medium are the topmost benchmarks of CDs over other probes. Same as numerous number of probes, there are countless analytical techniques were reported so far, for the recognition of metal ions which are atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), calorimetric, Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), and electrochemical performances [13]. Apart from previously stated approaches, fluorescence-based approach is highly suggested because all other techniques require extra time for the preparation of the analyte, and also for a single analysis, the cost of operation is comparatively very high. With the assistance of a fluorescence-based technique, the sample preparation and analysis time can be reduced efficiently and this approach yields high sensitivity and selectivity, even multiple analytes can be analyzed in a short period. Meanwhile, as mentioned, above CDs are less toxic and expose remarkable biocompatibility. Usually, cells easily uptake CDs without troubling the nucleus and also ties selectively toward the DNA system [14]. Generally, ruthenium complexes explore better anticancer activity, but the issues are preparation time and cost, recently Liangliang Yue and co-workers reported ruthenium to contain CDs for photodynamic cancer therapy [8].

In this present work, Ru (Ⅱ)-holding CDs were prepared from a comfortable, short time, and facile microwave-assisted technique with RuCl3.H2O as a metal source and ethylene diamine as carbon and nitrogen source. The bivalence oxidation state of ruthenium in the whole Ru@CDs structure was examined through different analytical methods particularly UV–vis and XPS studies, and Ru@CDs exhibits bright emission at 460 nm and explore its selective quenching ability toward Cr6+ and Hg2+ environmental toxic metal ions among numerous cations and anions. Moreover, it was also employed for the recognition of both metal ions individually in the environmental real samples such as lake and tap water, followed by the metal-doped CDs embedded in filter paper for a portable device for visual detection of metal ions was also successfully achieved. The excellent water solubility of Ru@CDs is further utilized for anti-cancer activity (bioimaging) against (THP-1) cells. This method paves way for the facile preparation of metal-based CDs and explores multifunctional applications.

RuCl3.H2O obtained from Sigma Aldrich, ethylene diamine, ammonium chloride, ammonium fluoride, ammonium phosphate, ammonium iodide, ammonium acetate, ammonium bromide, CuCl2.2H2O, NiCl2.6H2O, Zn(CH3COO)2.2H2O, SnCl4.5H2O, MnCl2.4H2O, LiCl2 CaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(CH3COO)2, CdCl2, FeCl3.6H2O, MnCl2.4H2O, NaCl, CoCl2.6H2O, and K2Cr2O7 were purchased SRL private limited. All the chemicals used here as without doing any additional purification. Milli-Q water was used for the whole experiments. All reagents employed in present work were purchased from SRL Pvt Ltd. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), Iscove's modified Dulbecco's medium (IMDM), and Trypsin were gained from Sigma Aldrich Co, St Louis, USA. EDTA, glucose, and antibiotics were from Hi-Media Laboratories Ltd., Mumbai. All the reagents established and used as without any refinement process.

Optical absorption properties were recorded with the aid of Carry-60 UV–Visible spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission studies are done with Horiba Fluoromax R928P. FT-IR spectral properties were processed by using Shimadzu IR Tracer 100 in ATR mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ULVAC-PHI 5000 probe Ⅲ XPS) was used to study the surface properties. Raman was obtained from (MicroRaman Spectrometer HORIBA, LabRam HR Evolution) Zeta potential carried with Malvern Panalytical X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern obtained from PAN analytical Aeris benchtop X-ray diffractometer furnished with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) as X-ray source. Fluorescence lifetime decay was recorded with Edinburgh instrument (FLS 1000). The high-resolution morphological studied with high-resolution transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2100 Plus HR-TEM). Fluorescent images were taken using CLSM microscope (LSM 700, Carl Zeiss, Germany).

Blue color-emitting Ru@CDs were prepared via microwave-aided technique. At first, 10 mg of RuCl3.H2O was taken with 10 ml of ethylenediamine in a 50 ml beaker and kept under stirring for 30 min, then the resultant reactant combination was set aside and then heated for 4 min at 700 W household microwave oven. The resultant dark brown color product was allowed to attain at room temperature, after that the product was dissolved in 15 ml of deionized water (Scheme 1 ). Ru@CDs in water were filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove the unreacted reactants, and the dark brown resultant solution was carefully stored at ambient temperature. Subsequently, Ru-free CDs were prepared by the similar procedure without adding Ru source with an extended heating time of exactly 9 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ru@CDs as a probe for quick detection of environmental pollutants
ce:section.ce:para: `The fluorescence emission feature of as-synthesized Ru@CDs was studied against a plentiful number of metal ions was examined with the aid of a fluorescence-based analytical system. Initially, a standard solution of metal ions (0.5 mM) was prepared individually. By the way, 4 0 μL of Ru@CDs suspension (optimized based on intensity) was taken with 3 ml of deionized water in a cuvette as a detection probe. A known amount of as-prepared metal ions was added to the equipped probe solution. After adding metal ions to the probe solution, the whole mixture was unwaveringly moved for recording the emission character of Ru@CDs using the fluorescence spectroscopic method without any break, analyte with probe solution was analyzed. The variation in emission intensity upon series addition of metal ions was documented at 460 nm via exciting at 380 nm. The impact of other metal ions and common anions were also studied in the presence of Hg(Ⅱ) or Cr(Ⅵ) heavy metal ions exclusively.

The optical stability of as-synthesized Ru@CDs was examined set against different environments such as altered pH conditions, ionic strength using various concentrations of NaCl, and continuous photo irradiation for a particular period, under the direct irradiation of UV light at 365 nm, and storage period for 30 days.

Detection of two different metal ions individually with the aid of filter paper, a low-cost visual detection sensor was also developed. Initially, a cellulose-based Whatman filter papers were cut into different pieces with the dimension of 1 × 3 cm approximately. The piece of filter paper was soaked into newly prepared Ru@CDs for 3–5 s, then the paper was dehydrated at room temperature, and the Ru@CDs – filter paper probe was then used for the detection of pollutants by increasing concentration of metal ions.

The prime optical property of metal-doped CDs was observed with the UV–Vis spectroscopy process. Relating the two different CDs, Ru@CDs led to a noticeable additional extensive peak attained at 448 nm. Fig. 1 a illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs and Ru@CDs recorded in a water medium, which depicts two different absorption bands at 272 nm are parallel to the π- π∗ transition that takes place in sp2 aromatic CC structure and 340 nm corresponds to the n- π∗ transition confirms the existence of C–N/CN groups present in the CDs [15,16]. Moreover, in Ru@CDs conspicuous minor absorption band at 283 nm, it may due to the π-π∗ transition in the sp2 hybridization in CC matrix followed by major absorption band arises at 448 nm owing to the metal-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) transition ascribed that the ruthenium metal ion may synchronized to the N-atom during the carbonization process [8]. One can note that the absorption band at 340 nm for undoped CDs was diminished are responsible for the functional groups may bound with ruthenium metal during carbonization process. Ru-metal and amine groups from the ethylene diamine may form a bond with each other and leads to the formation of complex followed by carbonization which was reflected in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3d).

PL spectroscopy is an effective tactic to study the photo response of fluorescence materials. Here, both CDs and Ru@CDs display excitation-dependent emission category followed by retort to a wide range of excitation wavelengths covering from 280 nm to 480 nm. On the other side, it is fascinating to note that the undoped CDs found from a single reactant called ethylene diamine also shows deep cyan-color emission (Fig. 1b), with a maximum emission intensity at 424 nm over the excitation wavelength of 340 nm, indicates the transition of multiple functional group moieties exists on the surface of CDs during the carbonization process, whereas exciting Ru@CDs at 340 nm display an emission band centered at 434 nm which was red-shifted than the undoped CDs (Fig. 1d). In addition, the emission maximum of Ru@CDs was noted at 460 nm exciting at 380 nm (Fig. 1c); results reveal that existence of Ru red-shifted to the maximum emission and their corresponding excitation energy compared to CDs. Discreetly, the emission intensity of the Ru@CDs is efficiently quenched with an increase in emission wavelength, which represents that Ru@CDs assembled with the Ru–N bond, followed by the electron transfer that takes place from the functional group moieties around Ru in Ru@CDs and also reduced the electronic transition of C–N/CN moieties [17]. Additionally, the average lifetime calculated from the fluorescence lifetime analysis. The average lifetime of the CDs was gradually increased from 7.02 ns to 9.36 ns, which was achieved by the fusion of Ru@CDs shown in Fig. 1(e and f). During the carbonization process increases the lifetime at excited state. From the PL lifetime spectroscopy, the rate of recombination was limited, after the introduction of Ru metal which was formed along with CDs-like skeleton followed by a shift in wavelength also noticeable, which was the result of a transfer of electrons from electron-rich domain species around Ru metal to the electron-deficient species Ru. Finally, the above UV–Vis, PL, and PL lifetime characteristic system confirm the Ru metal successfully synchronized to the CDs matrix during the one-step simple carbonization route. The quantum yield (QY) of CDs and Ru@CDs was calculated with the aid of literature [18], and QY was calculated from the emission wavelength of the samples by taking quinine sulfate as a reference through the ratio of the integrated photoluminescent intensity, with the exciting wavelength of 340 nm and 380 nm, respectively. The obtained absolute QY was 33.56% for CDs and 19.21% for Ru@CDs.

The size and morphology of the CDs and Ru@CDs were well-characterized by TEM images. The TEM results display that CDs (Fig. 2 b and c) look like spherical dot-like structure with an average size of 5.06 nm with interplanar spacing value of 0.33 nm, and the TEM and HR-TEM image of spherical dot-like structure marked CDs inserted in Fig. S1c, d. On the other hand (Fig. 2d and e), Ru@CDs show an average particle size of 6.04 nm, and the size between two different CDs is slightly varied may due to the presence of Ru. The interplanar spacing distance was calculated as 0.34 nm (Fig. 2f), which corresponds to the (002) plane reliable with disordered graphene, and same d-spacing value was also calculated from the formula (nλ = 2dsinθ). The XRD patterns of the CDs and Ru@CDs are displayed in Fig. 2a. The XRD spectra of CDs display a broad spectrum positioned at 22.6°, which is parallel to the (002) plane value. On the other hand, the XRD pattern of Ru@CDs shows a broad peak, same as CDs, but there was a shift in the broad spectrum compared to the CDs observed, and it was centered at 27.7° also corresponds to the plane value of (002). The overall information gained from the XRD analysis reveals that CDs and Ru@CDs are obtained as highly amorphous forms tailed by constructed with highly disordered graphitic structure. Interestingly, the observed shift in the Ru@CDs structure has evidenced that the introduction of Ru atoms is coordinated to the CDs-like matrix around Ru; during the carbonization process of Ru and ethylene diamine, there was no crystalline peak corresponding to Ru NPs, which was not found in XRD studies reveals that the Ru atom is coordinated to the CDs structure/matrix as small sized molecule-like structure, without any aggregation between the Ru atoms [19].
Here, the FT-IR measurement used to observe the functional groups in the CDs and Ru@CDs.

The peaks attained at 1120 cm and the sharp peak at 1384 cm are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–N group [3]. Meanwhile, the band found at 1595 cm was corresponded to the CC moiety; then, the small peak at 2902 cm refers to the CH2 assembly [20]. The above spectral ranges are proving that both materials were composed of rich functional groups. Finally, the broad spectra at 3327 cm in CDs represent the existence of the primary amine group, which was shifted to 3408 cm in Ru@CDs. The shift suggested that coordination has primarily occurred between the Ru and amine group (-NH) (Fig. 3 d). The as-synthesized CDs and Ru@CDs of molecular structural arrangement were further analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 3e shows the vibrational bands at 1300 cm (defective band) and 1480 cm (graphitic band) parallel to the CDs, which reveals the existence of a graphene-like structure [21,22]. One can see in Ru@CDs after introducing Ru, there was a red shift occurring at 1332 cm and 1506 cm; both the G and D bands were shifted to 31 cm and 26 cm [23]. The shift in both band structures represents that the defective and graphitic structure was altered due to the process of electrostatic interaction between ethylene diamine and Ru. Then, the reaction was forwarded by complexation, polymerization, and carbonization during microwave irradiation. This result supports that metal ions are coordinated to the CDs [23]. The deep investigation of the chemical composition of CDs and Ru@CDs was explored by the XPS technique. Fig. 3a portrays the survey spectrum of Ru@CDs. It is known that C 1s and Ru 3d peaks were obtained as an overlapped spectrum. There were four individual peaks found in the deconvoluted C 1s + Ru 3d spectrum (Fig. 3b). From this instance, C 1s spectrum produces two peaks at 284.7 eV and 288.29 eV corresponds to the CC and O–CO moieties [1]. On the other side, the doublet spectrum attained at 280.39 eV and 285.09 eV was parallel to the Ru 3d5/2 and Ru 3d3/2, and these values correspond to the Ru(Ⅱ) pyridinic moieties [24]. The above data confirm the existence of Ru, and it was coordinated to the nitrogen-based moieties as small-sized particles. Similarly, the deconvoluted N 1s spectra (Fig. 3c) show the presence of pyridinic-N, Pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N at 397.84 eV, 399.54 eV, and 401.24 eV [25]. One can see that, comparing the N 1s spectra of CDs (Fig. S2c) and Ru@CDs as-mentioned pyrrolic-N spectrum remains unchanged, but interestingly red-shift in the pyridinic-N and a blue-shift in the graphitic-N spectrum was found about the binding energy of 0.4 eV and 1 eV. The change in binding energy in the N 1s spectrum suggests the formation of Ru(Ⅱ)-polypyridine/Ru(Ⅱ)-graphitic nitrogen complexes during the carbonization process [25]. Even the Ru metal is supposed to form a complex between two different N-based moieties are confirmed but the precise coordination structure was still unknown. Meanwhile in Fig. S1(a,b), the zeta potential was analyzed as 3.19 mV for Ru@CDs and −11.7 mV for CDs; this evidence confirms that Ru was present in the CDs matrix and fluctuates the surface charge.

As mentioned in the experimental part, the stability of Ru@CDs was examined in different environments from that there was no major change in the PL intensity which was observed in the ionic environment with an extent concentration of about 200 mM of NaCl solution (Fig. S4a), then continuous irradiation of UV light wavelength of 364 nm continuously for 1 h (Fig. S4b), followed by the storage time of 30 days (Fig. S4d). But in the varied pH environment, the PL property was affected at lower pH; there was an enhancement in the PL intensity and constant decrease in PL intensity at higher pH regions. The enhancement of PL intensity with lower pH is due to surface of CDs will be highly in homogenous conditions [26]. Unfortunately at higher pH condition, the intensity falls down immediately this may be due to that positively charged Ru(Ⅱ) in the Ru@CDs could be reduced to its zero valence and then starts aggregation in the higher pH, so the emission intensity became reduced (Fig. S4c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of individual environmental toxic metal ions in an aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para:
The existence of highly active, and well-functionalized Ru@CDs with outstanding photoluminescent ability has been extensively utilized in a wide variety of applications. Particularly in this work, we employed Ru@CDs for the specific detection of multiple hazardous heavy metal ions selectively in an aqueous environment, which was successfully achieved with the aid of fluorescence spectroscopy-based analytical technique. To determine the fluorescence emission quenching capability of Ru@CDs in an aqueous state in contrast to multiple metal ions studied with a wide number of metal ions with a concentration of 150 μM, among the varieties of cations and anions studied, the probe selectively displays a better quenching of fluorescence emission intensity at 460 nm over the addition of Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal ions were plotted as an error bar graph. However, there was not much quenching of emission intensity observed with the other cations and anions addition. Finally, the recognition of heavy metal cations Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions is studied individually with Ru@CDs in an aqueous environment, both the heavy metal ion shows an outstanding linear quenching behavior with an increasing concentration range between 0 and 150 μM. During the specific recognition of Cr6+ ions with the Ru@CDs probe, the emission was entirely quenched (Fig. 4 a). On the other side, the quenching efficiency of the Hg2+ was comparatively low when compared to detection of Cr6+ with the probe (Fig. 4c). The constant quenching of fluorescence emission intensity over each addition of increasing concentration of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions onto Ru@CDs centered at 460 nm range was recorded and plotted as the concentration of Cr6+ vs. fluorescence intensity (I0/I) (Fig. S3a). From the plot, the entire addition of concentrations of Cr6+ ions into probe was considered for linear relationship value of (R2 = 0.9968), which was found from the detection range between 0 and 150 μM (Fig. 4b). The concentration of Hg2+ ions vs. fluorescence intensity (I0/I) was plotted (Fig. S3b), from a linear relation of (R2 = 0.98828) was observed for entire series Hg2+ addition among 0–150 μM concentration (Fig. 4d). With the aid of both linear-range plots, the fluorescence quenching constant was calculated from the Stern-Volmer equation as 2.14 × 104 M-1 for Cr6+ and 8.5 × 104 M for Hg2+ ions. The change in fluorescence intensity with a rise in the concentration of metal cation was fitted with the stern-Volmer equation (1).
(1)
I 0/I = 1 + K sv[Q]

where I and I 0 are the PL intensity of Ru@CDs in existence and the absence of Cr6+/Hg2+ ions cations at 460 nm. Q gives the concentration of Cr6+/Hg2+ ions, and K sv refers to the quenching constant. From the results discussed above, the cyan ucolor-emitting Ru@CDs probe was an excellent probe for the individual discerning multiple metal cations Cr6+ and Hg2+ in an aqueous atmosphere. Finally, the detection limit (LOD) was found as 0.128 μM and 0.328 μM for Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations, respectively, by using formula 3σ/m [1]. In Table S2, a comparison table was made and displayed detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations with different sensing systems.

At the same time, the undoped CDs were also examined to find out the detection capability toward the Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations; same protocol followed for Ru@CDs. The results are very interesting; actually the fluorescence from the CDs prepared from single precursor (ethylene diamine) was also responsive to the Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations. From this, it is confirmed that the detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations through fluorescence was originated from the ethylene diamine-derived CDs, and ruthenium in the CDs has enhanced the sensitivity toward the detection of individual Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations. Even though CDs can able to detect the Cr6+ and Hg2+ metal cations, but the PL quenching efficiency of CDs with Cr6+ was 41% (Fig. S6a) and Hg2+ was 39% (Fig. S6d), while Ru@CDs show the quenching efficiency of about 84% and 65% for Cr6+ and Hg2+ with the concentration of 150 μM. This incomplete efficiency of CDs reflects in the linear range and detection limits of the two different metal cations. For Ru@CDs, the linear range was found as 0–150 μM for both the metal ions; on the other side, CDs show a linear range of 31–94 μM and 31–134 μM (Fig. S6 c&f) for Cr6+ and Hg2+, respectively. Finally, the lower detection limit is also very low for CDs exhibiting 4.5 μM for Cr6+ cations and 4.8 μM for Hg2+ ions which was comparatively many times higher than the Ru@CDs. At the end, Ru@CDs not only explore the better PL quenching efficiency and LOD, other than that it also shows higher sensitivity and very good linearity compared to CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible assessments for enhancement in detection
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the enhancement in the detection of both metal cations increases curiosity. The detection efficiency of the Ru@CDs compared to the undoped ethylene diamine-mediated prepared CDs was enhanced awfully. Furthermore, the detection of Cr6+ boosted with the Ru@CDs compared to CDs. It is already described that Cr6+ was detected based on the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism means reabsorption of the incident light by the analyte. Actually, Cr6+ with Ru@CDs both have a positive net charge, so the interaction between the analyte and the probe will be minimal because of the fragile interaction. Cr6+ can easily absorb the incident wavelength light in the presence of probe (Fig. 6 left) and lead to quenching of fluorescence, also investigating another increasing intensity peak attained for Ru@CDs with Cr6+ at 448 nm similarly overlap with the emission spectra of Ru@CDs. Now it is proved that both the excitation and emission spectra of Ru@CDs were reabsorbed by Ru@CDs with Cr6+ mixture system. In CDs with Cr6+ system, only excitation spectrum was reabsorbed, so the obtained additional absorption range at 448 nm was responsible for enhanced detection of Cr6+ ions.

On the other side, the reason for the sensitivity improvement in Hg2+ detection may be, as debated in the mechanism for the detection of Hg2+ due to the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process referring to the transfer of a photo-induced electron from the probe to Hg2+ metal ions. PL and PL lifetime studies confirmed that the lifetime was reduced in Ru@CDs compared to the CDs due to the transferred electron in CDs origin to Ru; the same logic was applied here as the transfer of an electron from CDs to Hg2+ is very slow in CDs-Hg2+ environment because of quick recombination of an excited electron in CDs (Fig. 1e), so the transfer of an electron from the CDs to Hg2+ was hindered, so it reflected in quenching of CDs fluorescence. Likewise, the enhanced quenching of Ru@CDs with Hg2+ is due to the shrunken recombination of electrons, so this Ru holds CDs make electrons available for quite a long time; it was ratified with PL and lifetime analysis (Fig. 1f) compared to CDs, so the transfer of an electron from Ru@CDs takes place easily to the Hg2+ and quenched the fluorescence of Ru@CDs.

As mentioned above, the interference of other cations and anions was deeply investigated. However for practical applications, it is essential to retain its selectivity in presence of other foreign ions. To examine the selectivity property, the fluorescence response of the Ru@CDs probe with Cr6+ or Hg2+ ions in the presence of externally added individual cations and anions with a concentration of 150 μM was examined. Fig. S7a,b shows the error bar corresponding to the capability of Ru@CDs for the selectivity and sensitivity toward detecting Cr6+ or Hg2+ ions in the existence of other cations and anions. The green and red color error bar resembles the sensitivity and selectivity nature of the probe with other individual ions in the presence of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions separately and the blank indicates the sensitivity of Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions. From the output, it is understood that the Ru@CDs show better selectivity and sensitivity to Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions, particularly even in the existence of other foreign metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probable mechanism for the distinct metal ions discrimination
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the possible fluorescence turn-off mechanism for the recognition of specific metal ions, various studies are carried out. The PL quenching process may be occurred by static or dynamic quenching [13], which can be determined with the information obtained from the average PL lifetime decay of the probe in the presence and absence of individual metal ions. With the aid of the various experimental techniques, the possible fluorescence quenching progression can be determined. From that, we examined the different probable mechanisms such as Forster resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photo-induced electron transfer mechanisms (PET) [27]. Initially, we were involved in studying the possibility of the FRET mechanism for dual metal ions detection, for that we investigate whether there is any occurrence of emission from Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions under the illumination of UV light because of unobserved emission, possibility of FRET was neglected [28]. Next, we attempted for if there is any variation in absorbance range in the presence of Cr6+ and/or Hg2+ ions with the Ru@CDs. In presence of Cr6+ ions, there was a noticeable spectral change was observed at 347 nm and an increase in absorbance at 448 nm. On the other side, the excitation spectrum of Ru@CDs precisely overlapped with the absorbance spectrum of Ru@CDs (Fig. S5a), which confirms that the quenching was occurred due to IFE [29] and the mechanism cartoonized as Cr6+ was dragon which catches the incident light. The actual quenching of PL with Cr6+ occurred by reabsorption of excitation wavelength by the Cr6+ ions, so that the emission behavior of the probe was affected by lack of excitation light energy; by the way, PL was fully quenched with the increasing concentration which was picturized (Fig. S5a). The quenching process was further investigated with the aid of a lifetime decay experiment, in the presence and absence of Cr6+ ions. The average lifetime of the probe was remained unchanged after the addition of Cr6+ ions (Fig. S5c) from 9.36 ns to 9.22 ns results the turn-off fluorescence follows static quenching process and indicates the formation of the non-fluorescent complex. Also, we inspected for the formation of a complex between the probe and Cr6+ with the help of FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S8 red curve). But there was no evidence found from the above all results; it was again confirmed that the quenching of PL is due to inner filter effect. Although, in presence of Hg2+ ions, there are no changes observed in UV absorbance spectrum (Fig. S5b). Here, the quenching of PL in presence of Hg2+ ions by inner filter effect was omitted, and also there was no luminescence found when Hg2+ ions placed under UV light, which confirms that PL quenching was not dependent on FRET mechanism. Therefore, the PL was quenched through PET mechanism; for better support (Fig. S5d), average lifetime of Ru@CDs with Hg2+ ions was not affected, which remains the same for Ru@CDs as 9.36 ns and 8.49 ns for Ru@CDs with 150 μM of Hg2+. The altered average lifetime concludes that the PL quenching was a dynamic process and involved excited state interaction between the probe and the mercury ions. Then, we investigated for any interaction between them with the help of the FT-IR technique (Fig. S8 blue curve). There is no evidence obtained for the bonding/complexation between the Ru@CDs and Hg2+ ions, which supports that the quenching should be attained by the excited state PET [28] mechanism represented in the graphical image (Fig. 5) for better understanding of electrons depicted as butterfly and Hg2+ was picturized as a rose.

Now, it is essential to develop an easy, quick, and visual-based probe for detecting metal ions with a low-cost fabrication process for detecting harmful pollutants in the aqueous environment without any sophisticated analytical methods. For this, Whatman filter paper was employed as the stage for the visual detection probe; the Ru@CDs associated with the Whatman filter paper were detailed in the experimental part. The prepared paper probe dipped into the Cr6+, and Hg2+ prepared with different concentrations of 0 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM (Fig. 6a) and 0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 300 μM (Fig. 6b), respectively. The result found from the experiment shows that the fluorescence of Ru@CDs gradually quenched over increasing the concentration of metal ions. At 200 μM concentration of Cr6+, the fluorescence was quenched entirely was observed visually, and another side, the fluorescence nature of Ru@CDs was satisfied at the concentration of 300 μM. So far, probe filter paper displays excellent outcomes for the visual detection of metal ions rapidly. Finally, the Ru@CDs in simple, low-cost Whatman filter paper recognition probe will be an efficient, low cost, and rapid device in the near term.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr6+ and Hg2+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The detection potential of prepared sensor inspected with actual water samples such as lake water and tap water. Lake water collected from a lake at Potheri – Tamil Nadu, and laboratory tap water was acquired from our laboratory. The two different metal ions are prepared and spiked at the concentration of 10 μM, 20 μM, and 30 μM of prepared Cr6+ (Table S1a), and Hg2+ metal cations are spiked individually to the Ru@CDs solution (Table S1b). The recovery percentage was calculated and the obtained percentage for Cr6+ in the lake and tap water are from 99.5 to 102.3% and 99.6–100.1% followed by Hg2+ found as 98.2–99.6% and 97.3–99.9% for lake and tap water samples. Thus, the above results explore the feasibility of probes in sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs and Ru@CDs were tested on Human monocytic cell lines (THP -1) to inspect their ability to label the cells fluorescently. The labeled cells where excited with 408, 488, 555, and 639 nm lasers, and the emission was observed in the appropriate channels. A total of 20000 cells per ml were seeded in 1.5 ml Eppendorf tubes and treated with a sample at their MIC value for 24 h. After 24 h, the cells were pelleted at 1200 g, 25OC, and the supernatant was discarded. Untreated THP-1 cells were kept as control. These cells were treated only with PBS visualized in CLSM. The cytotoxicity of Ru@CDs was investigated through MTT assay. Fig. 7a shows concentration-dependent cancer cell killing efficiency of CDs and Ru@CDs from these results, Ru hold CDs efficiently inhibit the growth of living cancer cells, and it demonstrations 42% (2.5 μg/ml) viability after 24 h. Among the tested also studied the cytotoxicity of CDs (Fig. 7a). Excitation wavelengths, Ru@CDs-treated THP-1 cells indicated fluorescence only when excited at 488 nm (Fig. 7e). In all the other three tested wavelengths, THP-1 cells show appreciable fluorescence emission with only Ru@CDs above experimental protocol mentioned in supporting information. These results points that Ru@CDs was uptaken by cells easily compared to the CDs can't able to penetrate the cells, it is already reported that existence of highly hydrophilic substance like (-COOH, –NH2, –OH, etc.) cannot able to cross the phospholipid bilayer structure was present in the cell membrane, so it leads to insignificant imaging stage [30]. For instance, the rich hydrophilic functional group on the surface of the CDs (-NH2) group may inhibit the CDs from entering into cells. Somewhat, Ru@CDs cellular uptake was more incredible than CDs, which may be due to the existence of ruthenium in CDs with a positive surface charge of 3.14 mV, excellent fluorescence character, good cytotoxicity, and amphiphilic nature will facilitate the uptake of Ru@CDs toward the cells than CDs.

In conclusion, we have reported admirable water-soluble Ru@CDs was prepared by a simple, effective, and rapid microwave-assisted process using ruthenium chloride and ethylene diamine precursors for environmental and biological applications. The Ru@CDs explore excellent water solubility, fluorescence properties, and stability against different environments and are profoundly studied using various analytical instrumentation techniques. The environmentally risky metal ions Cr6+ and Hg2+ selectively turn off the emission of Ru@CDs individually in the aqueous condition through the different quenching mechanisms called IFE and PET with good selectivity and sensitivity. The enriched detection of environmentally toxic dual metal ions with Ru@CDs than CDs was deeply discussed, followed by a probe also utilized for detecting metal ions in the real water samples. Moreover, the Ru@CDs embedded paper-based portable device was fabricated for detecting metal ions for direct visual detection without any other sophisticated analytical technique. Not only environmental factor Ru@CDs explores its anticancer activity against (THP -1) cells comparatively better than CDs was also discussed. Because of the easy preparation, stable water-soluble ruthenium, and long-time excited state lifetime characteristics of Ru@CDs open ups the opportunity, it can be applied in various environmental and biological applications such as photocatalysis, drug delivery, and so on.

Arun Annamalai – Design and Performance of Experiments, Analysis of Experimental Data, Writing – Original draft. Kumaresan Annamalai – Conceptualization, Methodology, Reviewing and editing. Ramya Ravichandran – Conceptualization, Methodology, Reviewing and editing, Aswathy Karanath Anilkumar – performing anticancer, biological activities and data. Ganesh Munuswamy Ramanujam – Visualization, Investigation of biological studies. Sundaravadivel Elumalai – Visualization, Investigation, Project administration and Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the major instrumentation facilities NRC-SRMIST and HRTEM Facility at SCIF set up with support from MNRE (Project No. 31/03/2014-15/PVSE-R&D, Government of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2023.101536.
